did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (EfficientNet-B3 and MobileNetV3-Small) to perform melanoma classification on the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset, which is a dermatology/skin-cancer task.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: Although the journal section is labeled 'Review', the paper presents original experimental results, specifically the development and validation of a machine learning framework (DermAI-Melanoma) using the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset. It reports new quantitative metrics (e.g., 97% accuracy for EfficientNet-B3, 94% for MobileNetV3-Small) and describes specific methods (patient-level stratification, model training, browser-based deployment), which aligns with the definition of a research paper producing new results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An educational machine learning demonstration framework for plastic surgeons using open datasets
journal: Journal of Plastic, Reconstructive & Aesthetic Surgery
brief_summary: This study presents DermAI-Melanoma, an educational framework designed to demonstrate reproducible machine learning workflows for plastic surgeons using open datasets. Utilizing the SIIM-ISIC 2020 melanoma dataset, the authors implemented patient-level stratification to prevent data leakage and trained two convolutional neural networks: EfficientNet-B3 and a lightweight MobileNetV3-Small optimized for mobile devices. Both models were converted to TensorFlow.js to enable browser-based deployment, facilitating accessible clinical engagement with data science. The EfficientNet-B3 model achieved 97% test accuracy with an AUC-ROC of 0.98, while the MobileNetV3-Small achieved 94% accuracy with minimal storage requirements. The results indicate that these deep learning models perform comparably to dermatologist benchmarks reported in literature for melanoma detection tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task using the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset. The EfficientNet-B3 model detected 92% of melanomas.
The negative class in the binary classification task. The EfficientNet-B3 model correctly classified 96% of benign lesions.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task using the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset to distinguish between melanoma and benign lesions. The EfficientNet-B3 model achieved 97% test accuracy, detecting 92% of melanomas and correctly classifying 96% of benign lesions.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A public open dataset used for melanoma classification, serving as a didactic case study for the DermAI-Melanoma framework.

use.training: Used for training two convolutional neural networks (EfficientNet-B3 and MobileNetV3-Small) with patient-level stratification to prevent data leakage.
use.testing: Used for testing model performance; EfficientNet-B3 achieved 97% test accuracy.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: area under the receiver operating characteristic curve [AUC-ROC]

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper presents descriptive performance metrics (accuracy, AUC-ROC, F1-score) and compares them qualitatively to literature benchmarks without statistical significance testing.

SIIM-ISIC 2020.notes: Model performance was comparable to dermatologist benchmarks in literature on melanoma detection tasks in published studies (range: 65–79% with visual inspection alone, increasing to 82–91% with dermoscopy).

training_details.data_splitting: Patient-level stratification to prevent data leakage

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper states that images underwent augmentation as part of the preprocessing pipeline, but does not specify the exact techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping), probabilities, or ranges used.

conclusions: The DermAI-Melanoma framework successfully demonstrates that plastic surgeons can engage with machine learning and data science using open datasets without requiring specialist infrastructure.
Patient-level stratification is an effective method to prevent data leakage in melanoma classification tasks when using the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset.
The EfficientNet-B3 model achieved high performance (97% test accuracy, AUC-ROC 0.98) in distinguishing melanoma from benign lesions.
The lightweight MobileNetV3-Small model is suitable for mobile deployment, achieving 94% accuracy with minimal storage (< 5 MB) and fast inference (< 2 s) on standard smartphones.
The deep learning models' performance is comparable to dermatologist benchmarks reported in literature for melanoma detection tasks (65–91% depending on the use of dermoscopy).
Converting models to TensorFlow.js enables browser-based deployment, facilitating accessible clinical engagement with data science.
important_remarks: The paper is labeled as a 'Review' in the journal section but presents original experimental results and new quantitative metrics, which may cause confusion regarding its classification as a research paper.
The study uses the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset, a public open dataset, which limits external validation to this specific source and may introduce dataset bias.
Patient-level stratification was employed to prevent data leakage, a critical methodological strength for melanoma classification tasks.
The paper does not report specific data augmentation techniques, probabilities, or ranges, only stating that 'augmentation' was used.
Performance metrics (97% accuracy, AUC-ROC 0.98) are compared qualitatively to literature benchmarks (65–91%) rather than through statistical significance testing.
The framework is designed for educational purposes for plastic surgeons, focusing on accessibility and browser-based deployment rather than clinical-grade validation.
No statistical hypothesis testing was performed; results are presented as descriptive performance metrics.
